title: Find What Is Using a Local Dev Port
hook: Your dev server says port 3000 is busy. Ask macOS who is holding it.
problem: A local server will not start because another process is already listening on the same TCP port.
starting_state: A developer sees an error like EADDRINUSE or address already in use for port 3000.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reports the process listening on the port.
when_to_use: Use when Vite, Next.js, Rails, Django, or another local server refuses to bind to a port.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to inspect remote servers. It only checks the local Mac.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A row showing the command name, PID, user, and LISTEN socket for port 3000.

commands: printf '%s\n' 'COMMAND PID USER FD TYPE DEVICE SIZE/OFF NODE NAME' 'node 4242 uri 23u IPv6 0xabc 0t0 TCP *:3000 (LISTEN)'
printf '%s\n' 'COMMAND PID USER FD TYPE DEVICE SIZE/OFF NODE NAME' 'node 4242 uri 23u IPv6 0xabc 0t0 TCP *:3000 (LISTEN)' | awk 'NR==1 || /LISTEN/'

shorts.title: Port 3000 busy?
shorts.voiceover: When your Mac says a dev port is already in use, do not guess. lsof shows the process and PID that owns it.
shorts.caption: Find the process using your dev port.

linkedin.question: What is your fastest habit for debugging EADDRINUSE on macOS?
linkedin.cta: Save this for the next time your local server refuses to start.

youtube.description: Use lsof on macOS to identify which local process is listening on a blocked development port.

experiments.variant_b: Lead with the lsof command immediately.

title: Stop the Process Blocking a Dev Port
hook: Free a stuck dev port without hunting through Activity Monitor.
problem: A stale local server keeps listening on a port after its terminal window was closed.
starting_state: Port 3000 is occupied by a process that should no longer be running.
what_changed: The process IDs returned by lsof receive a normal TERM signal.
when_to_use: Use when you are confident the process on that port is your stale local dev server.
when_not_to_use: Do not use on ports for databases, sync tools, or services you do not recognize.
undo: Restart the stopped application or dev server if you killed the wrong process.
expected_output: Usually no output. Running the port check again should show no listener.

shorts.title: Free a stuck port.
shorts.voiceover: lsof can output just the PID, and xargs can send a normal kill signal. Only do this when you know what owns the port.
shorts.caption: Free a stale local dev server port.

linkedin.question: Do you prefer killing stale dev servers by PID, port, or process name?
linkedin.cta: Use this carefully when a local server leaves a port behind.

youtube.description: Clear a stale local development process from a macOS TCP port using lsof and kill.

experiments.variant_a: Explain the caution before showing the command.
experiments.variant_b: Show the command first, then explain the caution.

title: Show Your PATH One Entry Per Line
hook: Wrong Node, Python, or FFmpeg? Start by reading your PATH clearly.
problem: A tool runs from an unexpected location because PATH order is unclear.
starting_state: The PATH variable is a long colon-separated string that is hard to inspect.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command formats PATH for easier reading.
when_to_use: Use when Homebrew, npm, pyenv, rbenv, or Xcode command line tools seem to resolve the wrong executable.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof that a command exists. Pair it with command -v for that.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A numbered list of PATH directories in search order.

shorts.title: Read PATH clearly.
shorts.voiceover: PATH bugs are often order bugs. Split the variable on colons, number the lines, and see where your Mac looks first.
shorts.caption: Debug command resolution on macOS.

linkedin.question: How often do your toolchain bugs turn out to be PATH order problems?
linkedin.cta: Save this as the first check for weird CLI resolution issues.

youtube.description: Format the macOS PATH variable as a numbered list to debug command resolution.

experiments.variant_a: Mention Node and Python in the hook.
experiments.variant_b: Mention Homebrew and Xcode in the hook.

title: See Exactly Which Command macOS Will Run
hook: Before blaming npm, Python, or Git, check the binary your shell actually found.
problem: Multiple versions of the same command are installed and the shell may be choosing the wrong one.
starting_state: A command works differently than expected, often after installing Homebrew, nvm, pyenv, or Xcode tools.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints the resolved executable path and version.
when_to_use: Use when a CLI behaves like an old or unexpected version.
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on which alone for shell functions or aliases. command -v is more shell-aware.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A filesystem path such as /opt/homebrew/bin/node followed by a version string.

shorts.title: Which Node is running?
shorts.voiceover: When a command acts strange, ask the shell what it will run. command -v gives the resolved path.
shorts.caption: Check the real CLI binary.

linkedin.question: What command has bitten you most often because the wrong version was first in PATH?
linkedin.cta: Run this before reinstalling a toolchain.

youtube.description: Use command -v and a version check to confirm which CLI binary your macOS shell is running.

experiments.variant_a: Use Node as the concrete example.
experiments.variant_b: Keep the lesson generic across all CLIs.

title: Find Large Files Inside a Project
hook: Before committing, check whether a huge video, build artifact, or export slipped into your repo.
problem: A project folder contains unexpectedly large files that slow sync, backups, or Git operations.
starting_state: A project directory may contain large generated assets, media exports, or build artifacts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command only lists matching files.
when_to_use: Use before commits, uploads, backups, or when a project folder suddenly becomes huge.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to measure total folder size. Use du for that.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Paths to files larger than 100 MB, one per line.

shorts.title: Find huge project files.
shorts.voiceover: When a repo feels heavy, ask find for files over a size threshold. It is read-only and perfect before a commit.
shorts.caption: Spot large files before they spread.

linkedin.question: What large file type most often sneaks into your projects: video, zip, database, or build output?
linkedin.cta: Run this before pushing a repo that suddenly feels slow.

youtube.description: Use find on macOS to list files over a chosen size threshold inside a project.

title: Find Which Folder Is Eating Disk Space
hook: When your Mac is full, start with the biggest folders in the current directory.
problem: Disk space is low and the user needs to identify which project folder or cache is largest.
starting_state: The current directory contains multiple folders or files with unknown disk usage.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command calculates and sorts apparent disk usage.
when_to_use: Use in Projects, Downloads, Desktop, or cache directories to find big items quickly.
when_not_to_use: Do not run from the filesystem root expecting instant results. Start narrow.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A human-readable sorted list such as 4.0K ./notes and 1.2G ./video-export.

shorts.title: What ate my disk?
shorts.voiceover: Use du for human-readable sizes, then sort numerically by size. Start in the folder you suspect.
shorts.caption: Sort folders by disk usage.

linkedin.question: What is usually the biggest disk-space offender on your Mac?
linkedin.cta: Use this from a specific folder when storage gets tight.

youtube.description: Find large folders on macOS by combining du with human-readable sorting.

experiments.variant_a: Lead with low disk space anxiety.
experiments.variant_b: Lead with a practical cleanup workflow.

title: Flush macOS DNS Cache
hook: Changed DNS but your Mac still visits the old place? Flush the resolver cache.
problem: macOS may keep using cached DNS answers after a domain, hosts entry, or local network record changes.
starting_state: DNS records changed, but browsers or local tools still resolve the previous address.
what_changed: The local DNS cache is flushed and mDNSResponder is signaled to reload.
when_to_use: Use after DNS changes, hosts file edits, local domain testing, or VPN resolver confusion.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to fix authoritative DNS propagation. It only affects your Mac.
undo: No undo needed. DNS answers will be cached again naturally as you browse.
expected_output: Usually no output, though sudo may ask for a password.

commands: printf '%s\n' 'sudo dscacheutil -flushcache; sudo killall -HUP mDNSResponder' 'After: run a fresh lookup with dig example.test'

shorts.title: Flush Mac DNS.
shorts.voiceover: If DNS changed but your Mac keeps using the old answer, flush the local cache and signal mDNSResponder.
shorts.caption: Clear stale DNS on macOS.

linkedin.question: When DNS debugging gets weird, do you check local cache before waiting on propagation?
linkedin.cta: Keep this around for local domain and VPN troubleshooting.

youtube.description: Flush the macOS DNS cache and reload mDNSResponder after DNS changes.

experiments.variant_b: Emphasize the command and mention scope later.

title: Watch a Log or Build File Update
hook: Need to see whether a file is still changing? Let tail follow it live.
problem: A developer or creator needs to monitor a changing log, export report, or build output file.
starting_state: A text file exists and may receive new lines over time.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command keeps the terminal attached and prints new lines as they appear.
when_to_use: Use for local logs, generated reports, build output, and simple text-based progress checks.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it for binary media files or huge one-time analysis. It follows appended text.
undo: Press Ctrl-C to stop following the file.
expected_output: Existing tail lines followed by new lines as the file is appended.

shorts.title: Follow a log live.
shorts.voiceover: tail dash f keeps watching a text file and prints new lines as they arrive. Stop it with Control C.
shorts.caption: Watch logs update live.

linkedin.question: What local file do you tail most often while building?
linkedin.cta: Use this for quick live feedback without opening another app.

youtube.description: Use tail -f on macOS to follow local logs and text output as they update.

experiments.variant_a: Show Ctrl-C prominently in the caption.

title: Search a Log for Errors With Context
hook: A wall of logs is useless until you pull the error and the lines around it.
problem: A local log contains too many lines to manually scan for the relevant failure.
starting_state: A text log exists with possible ERROR lines buried among normal output.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints matching lines with line numbers and nearby context.
when_to_use: Use when debugging build logs, app logs, export logs, or CLI output saved to a file.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it for structured JSON analysis when jq would be more precise.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Matching ERROR lines with two lines before and after each match.

shorts.title: Find errors faster.
shorts.voiceover: grep can show line numbers and context. Dash C two gives you the match plus two lines before and after.
shorts.caption: Search logs with context.

linkedin.question: Do you usually search logs directly, or save command output to a file first?
linkedin.cta: Try context lines the next time a log is too noisy.

youtube.description: Use grep with line numbers and context to inspect local macOS log files more quickly.

title: Check a URL Without Downloading the Page
hook: Before opening a broken page in five browsers, ask the server for headers.
problem: A developer needs to confirm whether a local or remote URL returns the expected status, redirect, or content type.
starting_state: A URL may be down, redirecting, cached, or returning an unexpected content type.
what_changed: Nothing changes locally. The command makes a HEAD request and prints response headers.
when_to_use: Use for quick checks of local dev servers, redirects, CDN headers, and status codes.
when_not_to_use: Do not use when the server handles HEAD differently from GET. In that case, use curl -i.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only locally.
expected_output: HTTP status line and headers such as content-type, cache-control, and location.

shorts.title: Check headers fast.
shorts.voiceover: curl dash I asks for headers only. It quickly shows status codes, redirects, content type, and cache behavior.
shorts.caption: Inspect URL headers from Terminal.

linkedin.question: What header do you check first when a web page behaves strangely?
linkedin.cta: Use this before opening another browser tab.

youtube.description: Use curl -I on macOS to inspect HTTP response headers, redirects, and status codes.
